= Close

Support 
• Price below current price 
• Selloff likely to pause or reverse 
Resistance 
• Price above current price 
• Rally likely to stall or reverse

Inside bar 
■ High of bar at, or below, Hof prior bar 
■ Low of bar at, or above, L of prior bar 
Outside bar 
■ High at, or above, H of prior bar 
■ Low at, or below, L of prior bar 
Outside bars 
Inside bars

Scalper: Trader or fast money trader 
• Plan to take quick profit 
• Usually exit within 1- 5 days 
Swing trader: Investor 
• Plan to hold as long as trending 
• Sometimes for months to years

Many Buyers and Sellers: Price Is More Accurate 
■ Prices tend to be accurate and fair 
■ If you want to sell, 
and 1st buyer offers too little, 
other buyers will offer more 
■ If you want to buy, 
but are offering too low, 
no one will sell to you, 
unless you offer more

Goal of Every Market: Trading Range 
■ Fundamentals change constantly 
■ Fair price changes constantly 
■ Usually in tight range (Trading Range) 
Traders think price is too high 
Price quickly falls 
Traders now think price is fair

Goal of Markets: Force Traders to Trade 
• Purpose of market is to facilitate trading 
• One consequence is market moves to create 
maximum number of trades and traders 
• Market constantly moves in ways 
to make traders trade, 
even when they do not want to trade

~ t 
@Albrooksall

• When price falls too far: 
No one sells, so no trading 
Market has to move 
higher to find sellers 
• When price rises too high: 
Buyers stop buying 
Price has to move 
down to find buyers 
If AAPL is offered at $1 billion per share, 
no one would buy 
Since no trades at that price, 
trading volume would be zero! 
Price has to come down to find buyers

Market Needs More Sellers: Goes up to Find Them 
■ Traders know if price falls more, 
even fewer sellers 
■ Market wants maximum trading volume 
■ Will stop going down 
■ Usually will bounce to find enough sellers 
■ Sometimes going sideways is enough 
If bears see that rally is limited, 
will then be willing to sell again 
I I I . I 
More volume 
at higher price

Low volume at this price

Volume Divergence Often Leads to Rally 
Emini 1 min 
Volume 
I I I I I I I I I I I I I 
10 ·10 
10 ·20 
If volume less at each new Emini low, 
market senses sellers no longer willing to sell at these low prices 
Market wants maximum volume, but getting less as it goes down 
Has to go up to find price, where sellers willing to sell again

News Not Important: Reaction to News Is All That Matters 
Before an important announcement, 
TV experts predict market's reaction to news 
They are right only 50% of time 
Institutions know all possible news outcomes 
They make price neutral, before the announcement 
They adjust the price, to accommodate any outcome 
Price already reflects the news, 
before the news is announced 
If TV experts knew how to trade, 
they would not be on TV 
They would be trading! 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
!:111 'p 11 
@Albrooksall

Market Can: Rally on Bad News or Sell Off on Good News 
To say news is bullish or bearish is ignorant 
All that matters is how the market reacts 
If it goes up on bearish news, 
then more institutional dollars expected worse news 
When not so bad, they had to buy to make price 
appropriate for unexpected news 
If it goes down on bullish news, 
then more institutional dollars expected better news 
When not so good, they had to sell to make price 
appropriate for unexpected news 
60 . 
News is useless 
Price is truth 
0+110+• 
If there is bad news, 
but good price action, 
they buy

Report therefore, cannot be bearish 
if market's reaction was bullish 
Stock can also sell off, if it beats on 
Top and Bottom Lines

@Albrooksall

When the stock market falls sharply, 
traders want to reduce risk ("Risk Off"), 
by trading less volatile stocks 
They might trade consumer staples (like PG and KO), 
instead of highly volatile stocks, like technology stocks 
When the stock market rallies sharply, 
traders are willing to take more risk, 
to get more reward ("Risk On")

and are focused on high probability of reward 
They are more willing to trade highly volatile stocks, 
like technology or biotech stocks, 
to get even more profit

Breakout: Traders Decide Price Is Now Wrong 
■ At some point, fundamentals change 
■ Often without, or before news event 
■ Traders simply, and painfully discover that 
price is wrong! 
■ Market quickly moves to find new range

Breakout: Tells Traders That Price Is Now Wrong 
Example: Bull breakout above top of range 
• Prices keep going up 
• Do not reverse back into range 
Example: Bear breakout below bottom 
of range 
• Prices keep going down 
• No reversal back into range

Two Forces Control All Markets: Context and Momentum 
Context refers to all of the bars to the left 
Where is the support and resistance? 
Traders who look to sell at resistance, 
and buy at support are "Value Investors" 
They want to buy when market is cheap, 
and sell when it is expensive 
J (See Video 19 on Support and Resistance) 
,+, 
Resistance at top of channel 
Resistance at prior high 
Sell reversal down 
Support at prior low 
Buy reversal up 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
!:I,, 
1p 1. ~ 
@Albrooksall

Two Forces Control All Markets: Context and Momentum 
Momentum refers to the strength of the trend 
(See Video 15 on Breakouts) 
Sell bear breakout (BO) 
or pullback {PB) 
When momentum is weak, 
When momentum is strong, 
traders trade in the direction of the trend, 
expecting it to continue 
Buy bull breakout (BO)

What Is Forex (Foreign Exchange)? 
• Currencies trade against one another in pairs 
• Example, if you fly from NY to Paris: 
Go to foreign exchange shop 
Give them dollars, they give you euros

If EURJPY is quoted at 
123.02 Bid/ 123.03 Ask, then

• Receive 123.02 Yen, if sell one Euro 
• Difference is bid-ask spread 
("the spread")

How Much Is a Pip Worth: EURUSD? 
• 1 pip in EURUSD is 
quoted as 0.0001 US dollars 
• If trading 100,000 units, 
one pip = $10 US dollars 
100,000 X 0.0001 = $10 
■ Profit or loss is in dollars 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
~: 1rt~, l 
@Albrooksall

How Much Is a Pip Worth: USDJPY? 
1 pip in USDJPY is quoted 
as .01 Japanese yen 
■ If trading 100,000 units, 
one pip = 1,000 Japanese yen 
100,000 X 0.01 = 1,000 yen 
1000 yen= 1 pip

@Albrooksall

How Much Is a Pip Worth: USDJPY? 
Realized profit or loss is in yen 
Automatically converted to 
US dollars at end of session 
If USDJPY = 104.32, then 
1,000 yen = $9.59 = 1 pip

@Albrooksall

How Much Is a Pip Worth: 100,000 USDJPY? 
If USDJPY = 104.32, then 104.32 yen per dollar 
2 is the pip digit, so 10,432 pips per dollar 
1 pip= 1/10,432 dollars= 0.0000959 dollars per unit 
Standard trade size is 100,000 units, so 
1 pip for a standard trade = 100,000 x 0.0000959 = $9.59 
1 pip is about $10, when 
trading 100,000 units

Price Quote Example: 100,000 EURUSD 
SymbOI 
Last 
Bid 
Ask 
EURUSD 
1.113~-7 1.11347 1.11361 0.00014 
■ Bid= 1.11347 (price you pay to sell) 
■ Ask= 1.11361 (price you pay to buy) 
■ Broker's profit is bid/ask spread 
1.11361 - 1.11347 = 0.00014 = 1.4 pips= $14 
(100,000 units X 0.00014 = $14)

Forex Charts: Do Not Show Actual Trades 
Chart can be based on: 
• Bid 
• Ask 
• Bid and Ask 
(low of each bar is maximum Ask at the low, 
and high of bar is minimum Bid at the high) 
• Midpoint of Bid and Ask

Charts: Brokers Have Several Choices 
Chart based only on either: 
■ "Inside" Bid 
(Inside means the closest Bid to 
the last trade) 
■ "Inside" Ask 
(closest Ask to last trade) 
EURUSD 
Based on inside Bid 
(default for FXCM) 
EURUSD.A 
Based on inside Ask 
(Add ".A" to symbol) 
High= 1.13190 
High = 1.13205 
(1.5 pips higher,

Chart based only on either: 
• Midpoint of Bid and Ask 
• Minimum Bid and maximum Ask

of Bid and Ask 
High = 1.131450 
(6 decimal places) 
Low= 1.131375 
(6 decimal places) 
(default for Interaction Brokers) 
EURUSD 
High = 1.13165 
(max Ask) 
Low= 1.13125 
(min Bid) 
Based on minimum inside Bid 
and maximum inside Ask 
(bar is bigger since not using midpoint)

Chart Based on Bid: Buy Trade Might Not Be on Chart! 
EURUSD 5 min 
The default FXCM charts show only Bids, 
not actual trades or Ask 
0\ 
~t~~, 
M: Buy limit order 
Limit order to buy might not get filled here 
Buy orders filled at Ask, which is above Bid, 
and therefore above low of bar 
Only get filled if Ask falls below buy order 
Buys get filled at Ask, 
which can be above high of bar 
Can get filled on long above high of bar 
Might get filled on buy order, 
above high of bar 
Your trade might not be on chart!

Chart Based on Ask: Sell Trade Might Not Be on Chart! 
EURUSD 5 min 
FXCM has the option of 
creating charts showing only Asks 
Limit order to sell might not get filled here 
Sell orders filled at Bid, which is below Ask 
and therefore below high of bar 
Only get filled if Bid goes above sell order 
Sells get filled at BID, which can be below low of bar 
Can get filled on short below low of bar

Forex ''Scalp'': 1 - 5 Bars, Only Small PB 
EURUSD 15 min 
Most Forex traders don't use that term 
Those who refer to scalps, 
Call everything a "trade" 
are trading 60 min charts and smaller 
Scalp is 10 - 50 pips 
1.11900 
Exit with 10 pips 
profit for scalp 
All other trades are just "trades" 0

Swing: 5 or More Bars, Often 2 or More Legs, Allow PB 
EURUSD 60 min 
On 5 - 15 minute chart, scalp is 10 - 40 pips 
Here, on 60 minute chart, scalp is 20 - 50 pips 
Scalper exits with 
20 pips profit 
Anything more is "swing" or "trade" 
Swing usually at least 10 bars, 2 legs

Profit or Loss on a Trade: Based on Quote Currency 
Example: EURUSD 
• Based on the quote currency (here, USD) 
• Converted into your native 
account currency, at end of session 
• Since USD is account currency, no conversion 
1QQEURO 
HB 06938251 • 
••• 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
~:'n~, 7 
@Albrooksall

End of Trading Day: Converted Back to Your Currency 
If traded EURJPY, but native account currency US dollar 
• Automatically, at end of day: 
If profit, it is in yen, so yen sold at Bid into dollars 
If loss, buy yen at Ask in dollars to cover loss

Rollover: At End of Each 24 Hour Session (5 pm ET) 
• All positions are closed 
• Reopened automatically (rolled over) for next trading day 
(begins just few minutes later, except on Friday)

Carry Trade: Buy Currency with Higher Interest Rates 
Buy currency (or bond) with higher interest rate 
Sell currency ( or bond) with lower interest rate

Example: long 100,000 AUDCHF 
If roll rates $3.20 for longs and <-$6.10> for shorts: 
• $3.20 added to your account on open of next session 
• If Friday, get 3 days worth of interest, so $9.60 
• If you were short 100,000 AU OCH F, 
$6.10 x 3 = $18.30 would be debited

• Long trade profit or loss = 
(exit price - entry price) X conversion factor X trade size 
• Short trade profit or loss = 
(entry price - exit price) X conversion factor X trade size 
• Conversion factor = exchange rate to convert into US dollars

Your chart may be above the MA, 
because you are using 24 hour data 
(ETH - Extended Trading Hours) 
Your prices are different, because you are 
using quarterly contracts (not here), 
instead of continuation contracts

Indicator: 60 Min 20 Bar EMA (TradeStation) 
Inputs: 
_length( 20) , 
SessionEndTime(1315 {PST US Session}) ; 
If CurrentBar -
240 Then valuel = Xaverage( C, 240 ) ; 
If CurrentBar > 240 Then 
Begin 
If FracPortion(( Time - 30) / 100) = 0 or Time -
SessionEndTime Then 
Begin 
valuel = valuel[l] + (2 / (_length+ 1 )) * ( C - valuel[l] ) ; 
value2 = valuel ; 
Plotl( valuel , " 60 min EMA" ); 
End 
Else 
Begin 
value2 = valuel + (2 / (_length+ 1 )) * ( C - valuel[l]) ; 
Plotl ( value2 , " 60 min EMA" ) ; 
End ; 
End ; 
Note: Adjust 1315 to match session end time for your time zone. 1315 is for Pacific Standard Time.

@Albrooksall

Indicators: 15 Min & 60 Min EMAs on 5 Min Chart 
A close approximation to Al Brooks' programmed EMAs, 
can be achieved for any platform 
Adjust period of EMA within platform indicators 
• For 15 minute EMA on 5 minute chart (15min/5min = 3): 
Set EMA indicator period to 3 x 20 = 60 (50 is more accurate) 
• For 60 minute EMA on 5 minute chart (60min/5min = 12): 
Set EMA indicator period to 12 x 20 = 240 (220 is better)

Program Trading: Controls All Moves 
• Bearish programs fight to create 
downside breakout 
• Bullish programs fight to create 
upside breakout 
• Eventually one side wins, 
other side covers, 
and market breaks out 
Bulls give up 
Bears keep selling 
Bears give up 
Bulls keep buying

• 30% of trading is Arbitrage Trading 
• Firm takes opposite positions, in closely related 
markets, to make small profit on tiny discrepancies 
• Keeps related markets close to fair value 
• HTF firms monitor related markets for discrepancies, 
and look to make quick, small profit

Every Bar: Either a One Bar Trend or One Bar TR 
Every bar is either: 
• Trend bar 
big body, small tails 
• Trading Range bar 
Small body, big tails 
I

Trading Range bar

Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

• A small body in strong trend 
is more of trend bar 
• A bigger body in sideways market 
is Trading Range (TR) bar 
Big bars and bodies, but 
sideways market so TR bars 
Small bars, but 
3 consecutive bull bars, 
so early bull trend 
Close above middle, 
with no tails below, 
so bulls eager to buy

Slide 3 
@Albrooksall

Best Buy Signal Bar: Bull Reversal Bar 
Open near, or below close of prior bar 
Close well above prior bar's close 
Not much overlap with prior bar, or bars 
Good follow-through: 
Lower tail that is about one third, to 
one half height of bar 
Bars after signal bar have bull bodies, 
and close near high 
Small, or nonexistent upper tail 
Not doji, inside bar, or reversal bar 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 4 
@Albrooksall

Best Sell Signal Bar: Bear Reversal Bar 
Open near, or above close of prior bar 
Close well below prior bar's close 
Not much overlap with prior bar, or bars 
Upper tail that is about one third, to 
one half height of bar 
Small, or nonexistent lower tail 
Good follow-through: 
Bars after signal bar have bear bodies,

@Albrooksall

Strong Trend: Why Does It Pull Back? 
As trend continues, it gets far from stop 
Risk is greater 
Institutions must prevent risk from getting too big 
Popular way is to reduce position size by taking profits 
When bulls take profits, 
market pulls back, and goes sideways to down 
E?• 
□+~ 
o+t 
-- Stop 
--- Stop 
Once bulls confident that bears 
will fail to reverse bull trend, 
bulls buy again 
Now have tighter stop 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com

Deep Pullback: If Premise Still Good, Trend Continues 
Some PBs go all of the way to beginning of trend, 
and sometimes beyond 
As long as premise valid, trend still good 
Often rally 
above resistance 
in bear 
Often fall 
below support 
in bull 
Deep PB to DB HL MTR 
Held slightly above prior low

@Albrooksall

50% PB in bear means distance to stop (risk) is same distance . 
as to bottom of bear trend (minimum reward) 
Probability favors trend resumption, 
so traders like to sell on 50% PB in bear trend 
Traders buy 50% PB in bull trend 
I 
Basis for much of math for traders

Bar Counting: Count Number of Legs in PB 
For example: 
■ An ABC correction has 
two legs 
■ Triangle is PB with 3 legs 
in one direction (5 or more total) 
■ Don't worry that most patterns 
are not perfect

Slide 13 
@Albrooksall

PB in Bull Ends: Bar Goes above High of Prior Bar 
■ PB ends when current bar's high 
goes above high of prior bar 
■ This trend resumption up can fail 
■ Can get another 1- 2 legs down 
■ If get 4 or more legs down, 
then probably reversal, not PB 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 14 
@Albrooksall

PB in Bear Ends: Bar Goes below Low of Prior Bar 
■ PB ends when current bar's low 
goes below low of prior bar 
■ This trend resumption down can fail 
■ Can get another 1- 2 legs up 
■ If get 4 or more legs up, 
then probably reversal, not PB

Bar Counting: Count Trend Resumption Attempts or Legs 
• Count number of attempts 
to resume trend 
• Each resumption attempt in bear 
usually has bear bar 
• Same as number of legs 
in pullback 
• Each leg in bear flag usually has one 
or more bull bars

High 1 Bull Flag: Sideways to down in Bull or TR 
If high of next bar goes above current bar: 
■ Current bar becomes 
High 1 buy signal bar 
(High 1 buy setup, High 1 reversal, 
High 1 Bull Flag) 
■ Bar that goes above 
is High 1 entry bar

in larger pullback

Consecutive Bottoms: High 4, 5, or 6 
Consecutive bottoms can be High 4, 5, or G 
Each bottom attempt has 2 or 3 legs, 
so each is either High 2 or High 3

If 2nd attempt is also Wedge, get HG buy signal 
Strong bear BO below Wedge, 
so 2 or 3 legs down likely 
Bear BO and failed bottom so reset the count

HS 
Consecutive bottom attempts, have higher probability of reversal

Strong Bear Trend: Never Look for Bottoms 
Ll 
Ll 
Hl 
Hl 
Only look for Hl, H2 buy setups in 
bull trend, or Trading Range 
Never look to buy a strong bear trend! 
Strong bear trends always form Wedge Bottoms, 
but channel too tight to buy 
Ll 
80% of reversals fail in strong trends 
Channel too tight for major reversal 
Only get minor reversal, which is bear flag 
BO on higher time frame 
Great traders expect bottoms to fail 
Only look to sell, and sell for any reason 
Ll bear flag is good reason

Signs of Strength: Buying and Selling Pressure 
Bars often show that either 
bulls or bears are stronger 
• Price action favoring bulls is 
Buying Pressure 
• Price action favoring bears is 
Selling Pressure 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

Buying and Selling Pressure: Increase Chance of Trend 
• Bulls buy to make market go up 
• Bears sell to make market go down 
• Create footprints that indicate 
pressure 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 3 
@Albrooksall

• Clear that bulls are in control 
• Clarity is due to Buying Pressure 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 4 
@Albrooksall

• More bull bars than bear bars 
• Consecutive bull bars 
• Bull bodies are bigger than bear bodies 
• Bars closing near high and at least above midpoint 
• Bull Micro Channels (no PBs) 
• Tails below bars 
• Bull BO bars with gaps, and follow-through 
• Number of consecutive bull bars increases 
• Bull bodies getting bigger 
• Number of consecutive bear bars decreases 
• Bear bodies getting smaller

■ What are gaps?

Any space between current price 
and any support and resistance 
Gap between low 
and prior high 
■ H, L, 0, C of any bar 
■ Moving Averages 
• Trend lines 
■ Can be very minor

and highs of prior bars 
Gap between 
open with close 
of prior bar

since it closed 
on next bar 
Breakout (BO) 
test of sign a I bar

low and MA 
Gap between low 
and prior high

Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

Traditional Gap: Space Between Bars 
Gap up means low of current bar is 
above high of prior bar 
Sign of strong bulls 
Gap down means high of current bar is 
Gaps are common on daily charts of stocks 
Less common on weekly and monthly 
and on Forex charts 
Rare on 5 minute charts of liquid markets 
below low of prior bar 
,1-,❖"-tY 
Sign of strong bears 
¢ □t +rt. 
Tt 
,, 
l 
Gaps on daily charts of stocks are 
especially common in TRs 
Usually meaningless in TRs, 
just like any other reversal and breakout 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 3 
@Albrooksall

Gap early in trend is a break away gap 
Gap in middle of trend is often 
a Measuring Gap 
' 
09'T,+ 0 
+ 
Gap at end of trend is an Exhaustion Gap 
Not certain until it ''closes'' 
All other gaps are just ''gaps'' or ''common gaps'' 
Rally above top of gap 
means the gap ''closed'' 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 4 
@Albrooksall

Island top is gap up 
followed by a gap down 
Can lead to reversal down 
Island bottom is gap down 
followed by a gap up 
Can lead to reversal up 
For charts that have gaps, 
every island reversal is simply a reversal, 
and has no other special significance 
For charts that rarely have gaps, 
every reversal can be thought of 
as a variation of an island top or bottom 
Since most reversals fail, 
most islands do not lead to reversals 
Island bottom is 
simply reversal up 
! I 
- ~, t 
'

@Albrooksall

Gaps do not have to be around same price in islands 
Islands often have multiple gaps up or down

Often many ways to draw them 
Big island bottom

and high of prior bars

Bull Measuring Gap: Look for Measured Move Up 
■ Bull trend bar after bull has 
gone on for 5 - 30 bars 
■ High of some bar before is 
below low of another bar after 
(do not have to be adjacent bars) 
■ Often becomes Measuring Gap 
■ Often leads to Measured Move 
Middle of gap

above high of bar after

• Often leads to Measured Move

MAG: Moving Average Gap Bar in Bull Trend 
■ Bull with 20 or more bars above MA 
■ Finally has one or more bars 
completely below MA 
■ Gap between MA and high of bar 
■ Usually leads to final rally 
before correction attempt 
■ 40% of major trend reversals succeed 
(60% become bull flag or TR) 
Gap between MA 
and high of bar

Gaps Are Common: Every Trend Bar 
Every trend bar is an attempt to breakout (BO), 
and therefore is a gap 
Most BO attempts fail, and 
the Trading Range (TR) continues 
Most gaps have no predictive value, 
'r 
and no follow-through 
The chance of a trend is greater 
if the low of the bar after 
is far above the high of the bar before 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

Micro Gap: Look at High and Low of Bar before and After 
• 
For every trend bar 
■ Look at bar before and bar after 
I 
■ If they do not overlap, there is a gap 
,_ 
• 
J 
■ Odds of follow-through are greater 
,-
I

If Micro Gap Stays Open: Trend Is Stronger 
Pullback (PB) fell 
below bottom of gap 
Bulls no longer able 
to keep gaps open 
Bulls losing control 
Higher prices less likely 
Bear gaps staying open 
Lower prices likely

BO point

>

PB from BO 
(top of gap)

without pulling back below the BO point 
(bears could not close the gap), 
bulls buy more, and bears wait to sell 
This means that there are usually 
many more bars left to the trend

Every Bull Chart: 1 or More of These 4 Patterns 
Each pattern might be 1 - 2 bars, 
or 20 or more bars 
Breakout 
Tight Bull Channel

Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

Bull Breakout: No Pullbacks, Only Buy 
Breakout 
-- Stop 
No pullbacks, or just one pullback 
lasting only 1 - 2 bars 
Stop is far so risk is big 
Probability of higher prices is high 
Best to buy for swing, but scalp is ok 
Do not sell 
On smaller time frame, contains 
Tight and Broad Channels 
and Trading Ranges (TR) 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 3 
@Albrooksall

Tight Bull Channel: BO on Higher Time Frame, Only Buy 
Tight Bull Channel 
-- Stop 
Most pullbacks only last 1 - 3 bars 
Stays Always In Long 
Stop is far so risk is big 
Probability of higher prices is high 
Best to buy for swing, but scalp is ok 
Do not sell 
On smaller time frame, 
contains Broad Channels 
and Trading Ranges (TR) 
On higher time frame, 
it is a breakout (BO) 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 4 
@Albrooksall

Broad Bull Channel: Mostly Buy, but Bears Can Scalp 
Sometimes has bear legs, 
strong enough to reverse 
to Always In Short for some traders 
Even strong bear leg 
stays above prior Higher Low 
If PB reaches about 2/3rd5, 
probability for new high, is 
about same as for new Lower Low 
Ok to sell new high for scalp, 
if weak rally 
Best to buy pullbacks (PB) 
Broad Bull Channel

On smaller time frame, 
contains Trading Ranges (TR) 
On higher time frame, 
can be part of Tight Channel 
or breakout (BO)

Slide S 
@Albrooksall

Trading Range: Bulls and Bears Scalp 
Bulls buy low where risk is small, 
reward is high 
Bears sell near top where risk is small, 
reward is high 
Probability better for bulls near bottom, 
and better for bears near top, 
but rarely better than 50% 
Bulls and bears scalp, so reward is small 
Buy low, sell high, scalp! 
On higher time frame, 
can be part of channel or breakout (BO) 
Trading Range

Ok to buy new low for scalp, 
if weak leg down

Trading Range: Bulls and Bears Scalp 
Bulls buy low where risk is small, 
reward is high 
Bears sell near top where risk is small, 
reward is high 
Probability better for bulls near bottom, 
and better for bears near top, 
but rarely better than 50% 
Bulls and bears scalp, so reward is small 
Buy low, sell high, scalp! 
On higher time frame, 
can be part of channel or breakout 
Trading Range

Binary Decisions: Easier to Decide When Just 2 Choices 
Decisions are easier when create 
only two choices (binary decision) 
For example: 
• Trend or Trading Range? 
• Breakout or channel? 
• Is channel tight or broad? 
• Major or minor reversal? 
No consecutive big bull trend bars 
Disappointing follow-through after each trend bar, 
and after new high 
Many big bear bars with close below middle 
Bears make money selling new highs 
Prominent tails 
PBs overlap BO points (all gaps closed) 
Conclude it is bull channel, 
and probably bull leg, in 
what will become TR 
Scalp at least part

Do not lower stop yet

Better to use other term to describe trend

Trending Trading Ranges: Range Usually Doubles 
• Market forms Trading Range (TR) 
for 10 - 30 bars 
• Range often about 
half of average daily range 
• Expect approximately 
a doubling of range 
• Breaks out briefly for 1 - 5 bars 
• Enters another TR 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 8 
@Albrooksall

Trending Trading Range: 60°/o of Minor Reversal 
■ Ranges often overlap 
TRs usually go beyond 
support and resistance, 
before reversing 
■ Like all TRs, 
can get BO in either direction 
60% chance that 
lower range will rally enough 
to overlap of upper ranges 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide g 
@Albrooksall

Trending Trading Ranges: Can Get 3 or More 
• BO can last many bars, 
but usually 3 - 6 bars before new TR 
• Sometimes can have 
more BOs and TRs 
• Market searching for fair price 
New BO, market deciding 
that price is stil I too low 
Needs to go higher to find sellers 
3rd TR overlapped the 2nd 
Trend is weakening 
2nd TR did not overlap 1st 
Gap, so strong BO 
Higher prices likely

Tight Channel: Trade Like a Breakout 
Sometimes difficult to know if 
breakout (BO) or Tight Channel 
• Assume BO and trade it like one 
• Tight Channel is a BO 
on a higher time frame 
Clearly bull trend so good to buy 
Should trader also sell? 
Cannot lose money if 
do not take short trades 
Odds of profitable 
short trade are low 
Only look to buy

Weak Bears: Get Trapped into Losing Shorts 
• Never trade against trend 
• PBs too small to make profit 
• Bears have tight stops so low risk 
• Low risk means low probability! 
• Small losses add up to big loss 
Bear reversal, but 
DO NOT SELL!

Weak Bulls: Afraid to Buy and Get Trapped Out 
■ Weak bulls too afraid to buy 
■ Want strong signal bar 
■ Want PB so can use tight stop, and 
have smaller risk 
■ Don't realize probability is 
very high IF using correct stop 
Beginners think buy setups 
are low probability and high risk 
Stop is far, but probability is high!

Most Breakouts Fail: My 80°/o Inertia Rule 
• Only 10% of bars are in a strong breakout, 
that has follow-through 
• 90% of bars, including trend bars, 
are in channels or TR 
• In channel or TR, 
80% of breakouts fail to go far 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 10 
@Albrooksall

• Traders buy weak High 1 Bull Flag, 
hoping for successful bull breakout 
• Usually more traders believe opposite, 
and are selling 
• Most breakout attempts fail 
High 1 Bull Flag, 
but doji signal bar, and 
after strong bear reversal bar 
Might be more sellers above 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 11 
@Albrooksall

Failed Bull Breakout: Can Fail and Become Bull Flag 
• Traders sell failed bull breakout, 
hoping for reversal down 
• Reversal down is a bear breakout 
Here, it is weaker than bull BO 
• Usually more traders believe opposite, 
and are buying weak sell signal 
• Most breakout attempts fail 
Failed bull breakout, 
strong bear inside bar, but 
might not be strong enough, 
to reverse strong bull bar 
Might be more buyers below

Breakouts: Most Breakouts Fail to Go Far 
If a trader believes that 
• Buyers will win above, or below a bar, 
he should buy 
• Sellers wil I win, he should sell 
• If uncertain, wait

Main Points: Signs of Strong Breakouts 
■ 2nd Leg Traps 
■ Signs of strong breakouts 
■ Succeed or reverse? 
Slide 
2 
8 
14 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 7 
@Albrooksall

Signs of Strong Breakout: Photographic Memory Test 
Be ready! 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 8 
@Albrooksall

• 
The breakout bar has a large bull trend body and small tails or no tails. The larger the bar, the more likely the breakout will succeed. 
• 
If the volume is ten to twenty times the average volume of recent bars, the chance of follow-through buying and a possible 
measured move increases. 
• 
The breakout goes very far, lasts several bars, and breaks several resistance levels like the moving average, prior swing highs, and 
trend lines, and each by many ticks. 
• 
As the first bar of the breakout bar is forming, it spends most of its time near its high and the pullbacks are small (less than a quarter 
of the height of the growing bar). 
• 
There is a sense of urgency. You feel like you have to buy but you want a pullback, yet it never comes. 
• 
The next two or three bars also have bull bodies that are at least the average size of the recent bull and bear bodies. Most bars have 
closes above their midpoints. Even if the bodies are relatively small and the tails are prominent, if the follow-through bar (the bar 
after the initial breakout bar} is large, the odds of the trend continuing are greater. 
• 
The spike grows to five to ten bars without pulling back for more than a bar or so. 
• 
As a bull breakout goes above a prior significant swing high, the move above the high goes far enough for a scalper to make a profit if 
he entered on a stop at one tick above that swing high. 
• 
One or more bars in the spike has a low that is at or just one tick below the close of the prior bar. 
• 
One or more bars in the spike has an open that is above the close of the prior bar. 
• 
One or more bars in the spike has a close on its high or just one tick below its high. 
• 
The low of the bar after a bull trend bar is at or above the high of the bar before the bull trend bar, creating a micro gap, which is a 
sign of strength. These gaps sometimes become measuring gaps. Although it is not significant to trading, they probably represent the 
space between a smaller time frame Elliott wave 1 high and a wave 4 pullback, which can touch but not overlap. 
• 
The overall context makes a breakout likely, like the resumption of a trend after a pullback, or a higher low or lower low test of the 
bear low after a strong break above the bear trend line. 
• 
The market has had several strong bull trend days recently. 
• 
There was growing buying pressure in the trading range, represented by many large bull trend bars and the bull trend bars were 
clearly more prominent than the bear trend bars in the range. 
• 
The first pullback occurs only after three or more bars of breaking out. 
• 
The first pullback lasts only one or two bars and it follows a bar that is not a strong bear reversal bar. 
• 
The first pullback does not reach the breakout point and does not hit a breakeven stop (the entry price). 
• 
The breakout reverses many recent closes and highs. For example, when there is a bear Channel and a large bull bar forms, this 
breakout bar has a high and close that are above the highs and closes of five or even twenty or more bars. A large number of bars 
reversed by the close of the bull bar is a stronger sign than a similar number of bars reversed by its high. 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 9 
@Albrooksall

• 
The breakout bar has a small or average size bull trend body and a large tail on top. 
• 
The next bar has a bear body and is either a bear reversal bar or a bear inside bar, and that bar closes on or near its low 
and the body is about the size of the average bodies of the bars before the breakout (not just a one tick tall bear body). 
• 
The overall context makes a breakout unlikely, like a rally to test the high of a trading range day, but the rally has bear 
bars, many overlapping bars, bars with prominent tails, and a couple of pullbacks along the way. 
• 
The market has been in a trading range for several days. 
• 
It is a bull breakout above a bull channel, coming after 20 or more bars in the bull trend, and it is the biggest bull bar in 
20 bars 
• 
The bar after the breakout bar is a strong bear reversal bar or a bear inside bar. 
• 
The bar after a bull trend bar has a low that is below the high of the bar before the bull trend bar. 
• 
The first pullback occurs two bars after the reversal. 
• 
The pullback extends for several bars. 
• 
The trend resumption after the pullback stalls and the market forms a lower high with a bear signal bar. 
• 
The spike breaks above a resistance level like a swing highj a bear trend line, or a bull trend Channel line by only a tick or 
so and then reverses down. 
• 
The spike barely breaks above a single resistance level but pulls back before breaking above other levels that are just a 
little higher. 
• 
A trader who bought on a stop above a prior swing high would not be able to make a scalper's profit before there was a 
pullback. 
• 
As the breakout bar is forming, it pulls back more than two thirds of the height of the bar. 
• 
As the breakout bar is forming, it pulls back for at least a third of its height two or more times. 
• 
The pullback falls below the breakout point. There are no gaps between the low of any bar and the high of the bar two 
bars earlier. 
• 
The pullback falls below the low of the first bar of the spike. 
• 
The pullback hits the breakeven stop. 
• 
There is a sense of confusion. You feel like you are not certain that the breakout will succeed or fai l. 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 10 
@Albrooksall

• 
The breakout bar has a large bear trend body and small tails or no tails. The larger the bar, the more likely the breakout will succeed. 
• 
If the volume is ten to twenty times the average volume of recent bars, the chance of follow-through selling and a possible measured 
move down increases. 
• 
The breakout goes very far, lasts several bars, and breaks several support levels like the moving average, prior swing lows, and trend 
lines, and each by many ticks. 
• 
As the first bar of the breakout bar is forming, it spends most of its time near its low and the pullbacks are small (less than a quarter 
of the height of the growing bar). 
• 
There is a sense of urgency. You feel like you have to sell but you want a pullback, yet it never comes. 
• 
The next two or three bars also have bear bodies that are at least the average size of the recent bull and bear bodies. Most bars have 
closes below their midpoints. Even if the bodies are relatively small and the tails are prominent, if the follow-through bar (the bar 
after the initial breakout bar) is large, the odds of the trend continuing are greater. 
• 
The spike grows to five to ten bars without pulling back for more than a bar or so. 
• 
As a bear breakout goes below a prior significant swing low, the move below the low goes far enough for a scalper to make a profit if 
he entered on a stop at one tick below that swing low. 
• 
One or more bars in the spike has a high that is at or just one tick above the close of the prior bar. 
• 
One or more bars in the spike has an open that is below the close of the prior bar. 
• 
One or more bars in the spike has a close on its low or just one tick above its low. 
• 
The high of the bar after a bear trend bar is at or below the low of the bar before the bear trend bar, creating a micro gap, which is a 
sign of strength. These gaps sometimes become measuring gaps. Although it is not significant to trading, they probably represent the 
space between a smaller time frame Elliott wave 1 low and a wave 4 pullback, which can touch but not overlap. 
• 
The overall context makes a breakout likely, like the resumption of a trend after a pullback, or a lower high or higher high test of the 
bull high after a strong break below the bull trend line. 
• 
The market has had several strong bear trend days recently. 
• 
There was growing selling pressure in the trading range, represented by many large bear trend bars, and the bear trend bars were 
clearly more prominent than the bull trend bars in the range. 
• 
The first pullback occurs only after three or more bars of breaking out. 
• 
The first pullback lasts only one or two bars and it follows a bar that is not a strong bull reversal bar. 
• 
The first pullback does not reach the breakout point and does not hit a breakeven stop (the entry price). 
• 
The breakout reverses many recent closes and lows. For example, when there is a bull Channel and a large bear bar forms, this 
breakout bar has a low and close that are below the lows and closes of five or even twenty or more bars. A large number of bars 
reversed by the close of the bear bar is a stronger sign than a similar number of bars reversed by its low. 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 11 
@Albrooksall

• 
The breakout bar has a small or average size bear trend body and a large tail on bottom. 
• 
The next bar has a bull body and is either a bull reversal bar or a bull inside bar, and that bar closes on or near its high, 
and the body is about the size of the average bodies of the bars before the breakout (not just a one tick tall bull body). 
• 
The overall context makes a breakout unlikely, like a selloff to test the low of a trading range day, but the selloff has bull 
bars, many overlapping bars, bars with prominent tails, and a couple of pullbacks along the way. 
• 
The market has been in a trading range for several days. 
• 
It is a bear breakout below a bear channel, coming after 20 or more bars in the bear trend, and it is the biggest bear bar 
in 20 bars 
• 
The bar after the breakout bar is a strong bull reversal bar or a bull inside bar. 
• 
The bar after a bear trend bar has a high that is above the low of the bar before the bear trend bar. 
• 
The first pullback occurs two bars after the reversal. 
• 
The pullback extends for several bars. 
• 
The trend resumption after the pullback stalls, and the market forms a higher low with a bull signal bar. 
• 
The spike breaks below a support level like a swing low, a bull trend line, or a bear trend Channel line by only a tick or so 
and then reverses up. 
• 
The spike barely breaks below a single support level but pulls back before breaking below other levels that are just a 
little lower. 
• 
A trader who shorted on a stop below a prior swing low would not be able to make a scalper's profit before there was a 
pullback. 
• 
As the breakout bar is forming, it pulls back more than two thirds of the height of the bar. 
• 
As the breakout bar is forming, it pulls back for at least a third of its height two or more times. 
• 
The pullback rallies above the breakout point. There are no gaps between the high of any bar and the low of the bar two 
bars earlier. 
• 
The pullback rallies above the high of the first bar of the spike. 
• 
The pullback hits the breakeven stop. 
• 
There is a sense of confusion. You feel like you are not certain that the breakout will succeed or fai l.

BrooksPriceAction.com

@Albrooksall

bars, not just scalp 
• Confident of higher prices, 
so willing to hold for swing trade

profitable, so trader satisfied

Trapped Emini Scalpers: 5 Tick Traps (and 9, 13, and 17 Tick) 
■ Emini scalpers often take 
1, 2, 3, or 4 points profits 
( 4, 8, 12, or 16 ticks) 
■ Creates support & resistance (5/R) 
■ If enter on stop, 
need 6, 10, 14, or 18 tick move 
■ If moves 1 tick less, traps traders

Bad Symmetry: Too Few Bars to Bar 3 
• Need 2 points to draw line 
• Count number of bars from 
bar 1 to bar 2, and from 2 to 3 
• Computers will ignore line if 
space between 2nd and 3rd points is 
too small (unless PB before 3) 
• If 3 - 5 times more bars before bar 2 
than between 2 and 3, and no PB 
between 2 and 3, ignore the line 
32 bars between bars 1 and 2 
Only 3 bars between 2 and 3 
Computers will ignore 
this 3, and look at it, 
as part of high at 2 
1 
3 
Real 3 will 
follow PB

@Albrooksall

Both Bull and Bear Channels: Trade Reversals 
Top of channel: 70% chance of reversal down 
Bulls sell to take profits on longs 
Bears sell to initiate shorts 
Bottom of channel: 
70% chance reversal up 
Bulls buy to initiate longs 
Bears buy back shorts 
to take profits 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 19 
@Albrooksall

Trade Reversals: Near Top and Bottom of Channel 
• Strong legs need 
strong reversal setups 
• If not strong setup, need either: 
2nd entry 
Strong BO on the reversal 
Strong signal bar not enough 
to reverse 3 strong bull bars 
Wait for 2nd signal to sell

channel

below channel, redraw line

(more horizontal) 
• Channel gets broader 
(lines further apart)

channel

Channel Bottom: Watch for Breakout or Reversal 
EURUSD 5 min 
When bull trend reverses down to test bottom of channel, 
there are often several possible bottoms 
Different computers will select different lines 
Impossible to know until reversal up, 
which will be most important 
Be aware of all, and watch how the leg 
down acts when near a possible bottom 
Look for reversal up or bear breakout

Broad Bull Channel: Like a Trading Range That Is Sloped 
All channels are trends 
Broad Channels are weaker trends 
Similar to TR that is sloped 
Can trade in both directions 
Minor HL 
Major HL 
Broad Bull Channel 
Bulls will buy for swings and scalps 
Bears can sell for scalps 
Selloffs can be strong enough to reverse to Always In Short 
Selloff makes Higher Low above prior Major Low 
(bottom of bull BO, not just bottom of channel)

Bull Channel: Transition into Trading Range 
As bulls become less certain: 
■ Scalp more and swing less 
■ Begin to take profits above prior high 
■ Losing confidence in prices 
going much higher 
■ Channel evolves into TR 
i 
Bulls selling at new high! 
Instead of buying BO, 
bulls taking profits 
Bulls buying pullbacks (PB) 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com

■ Bears doubt will go much higher 
■ Sell above prior highs, and scale in 
higher for scalps 
Bears selling above prior 
high for scalps

As bears become less certain 
■ Scalp more and swing less

prior low

going much lower 
■ Channel evolves into TR

bears taking profits

mostly sideways

twice size of average bar 
• If average bar is about 8 ticks tall, 
then PBs less than 16 ticks 
means channel is tight

Micro Channel: Look at Micro Trend Line 
Trend line from Micro Channel, 
which can be only 2 to 10 bars 
Most of bars touch, or 
are close to trend line 
Bars usually are relatively small 
Highlight small strong legs, 
where there are no pullbacks 
They are Micro Channels, 
so each is small trend 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 4 
@Albrooksall

Micro Trend Line: 1st Breakout Usually Fails 
Bulls buy 1st break below 
strong bull MC, expecting 
at least one more small leg up 
Can buy with limit order 
Bears sell 1st break above 
strong bear MC, expecting at least 
one more small leg down 
Can sell with limit order

Micro Channel Line 
is opposite from Micro Trend Line

Bull Micro Channel: Sign of Buying Pressure 
• No pullbacks means bulls buying 
above low of prior bar, 
so eager to buy at, or below bar 
• Don't sell 
Best bears can hope for is TR

Bear Micro Channel: Sign of Selling Pressure 
• No pullbacks is bears selling 
below high of prior bar, 
so eager to sell at or above bar 
• Don't buy 
Best bulls can hope for is TR

Bull MC: Bear BO Is Usually Bull Flag 
In bull trend 
■ Bull Micro Channel is Breakout 
■ Bear MC is PB, bull flag, and 
reversal attempt 
■ Most reversal attempts fail 
■ Expect bull to resume 
2nd entry buy (Micro DB), good signal bar 
BO PB from BO above MC 
Smaller time frame LL MTR 
0 
Common to see 2nd Leg Down, 
since MC is strong bear leg 
1st reversal up usually fails 
0
If buy (better to not buy above bear bar), 
use wide stop below bull leg

■ Most reversal attempts fail 
■ Expect bear to resume

between bulls and bears

Trading Range (TR): Broad Definition 
■ Any area of two-sided trading 
is Trading Range 
■ Even if it lasts for only single bar 
• Trading Ranges lasting 
only one bar are common 
■ Small Trading Ranges are 
called "pullbacks" 
One bar pause is PB, 
which is small TR

Pullback More Than 20 Bars: TR, Neutral 
Bear trend 
Pullback in bear trend has grown, 
to more than 20 bars 
No longer bear 
Has evolved into Trading Range

Chance of bull breakout, now 
same as for bear breakout

Pullback: Small TR, Traders Believe Trend Will Resume 
As long as traders believe that trend will resume soon, 
Trading Range is a pullback 
Only trade in direction of trend 
~Tt1iliQ 
09

■•□ 
Once unclear if trend will resume, pullback has grown into Trading Range 
Happens when there are both reasonable buy and sell setups 
No longer use term "pullback"

Bull Trend: Once below Stop, Then TR 
Bull trend needs Higher Highs and Lows 
After each new strong BO to a new high, 
swing bulls raise their stops 
to below most recent Higher Low 
(Major Higher Low) 
■•□ New low, but prior new high, ./' 
was not strong BO 
•"'Tt19Q 
09 
o+tOlo.+□llr;>oO 
TR often has Minor Lows or Highs 
Once leg down falls below Major Higher Low 
(low that is followed by strong BO to new high), 
bull trend has ended 
Now, either TR or bear trend

TR: BLSHS (Buy Low, Sell High, Scalp) 
In TR, 80% of breakout attempts will fail, so 
BLSHS: Buy Low, Sell High, Scalp 
Scalp, don't swing 
Most breakout attempts will fail 
Most moves will soon reverse back to entry 
tt 
Beginners always hopeful for trend 
They buy BO attempts at top, and 
sell BO attempts at bottom 
They swing trade, and watch profits 
turn into losses

Tight Trading Range (TTR): Dead Money 
• Sometimes hear stock market 
traders mention ''dead money'' 
• After big move on daily, 
usually down 
• Goes sideways for long time 
Tight Trading Range 
When stock falls sharply on daily, weekly, 
or monthly chart, often goes sideways, 
for 20 or more bars, 
in a Tight Trading Range 
Tight Trading Range (TTR) is common on 
all time frames in all markets 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

• Has to base for long time, 
before rally again 
• Takes long time for bulls 
to get same probability of bears 
• Very little potential for profit 
No return on investment, 
so "Dead money" 
Trade elsewhere 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 3 
@Albrooksall

• Trading Range, so BLSHS 
• If scalper cannot make money, 
buying near bottom, or 
selling near top, 
using stop orders to enter, 
then the TR is tight 
Difficult to make money, 
selling high or buying low, 
when entering with stops 
TR is therefore tight

Just Above Support in TR: Bulls Stop Buying until Reached 
Just above bottom of TR, 
both bulls and bears see bottom as support 
Institutions believe that market will test 
a little lower, and reach bottom of range 
Bulls stop buying, and 
wait to buy lower 
Bears also expect test, 
and wait to buy back 
shorts

Slide 11 
@Albrooksall

Just Above Support in TR: Bears Sell Strongly until Reached 
Both believe market will go a little lower 
Does not make sense for them to buy until 
market gets there 
This creates sell imbalance 
Market races down, and 
creates one or more big bear bars 
Quickly vacuumed down to support

just below top of the range

take profits there

@Albrooksall

At top, some strong bears are very confident of reversal down 
They will buy just below top, trying to create strong Buy Climax 
They then sell to create a reversal down 
Bears buying just below top, 
to make bulls think bull BO will succeed 
Want to trap bulls into buying too high 
Those bulls will have to sell with loss, after reversal down 
Mostly computers, 
since traders 
not fast enough 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 18 
@Albrooksall

At bottom, some strong bulls are very confident of reversal up 
They will sell just above bottom, trying to create strong Sell Climax 
They then buy to create a reversal up 
Bulls selling just above bottom, 
to make bears to think bear BO will succeed 
Want to trap bears into selling too low 
Those bears will have to buy with loss, after reversal up 
Mostly computers 
since traders 
not fast enough

Trapped Traders: Exit with Loss, Then Wait 
Breakout attempts look strong, but fail 
No follow-through, then get reversal 
Trapped bears exit with loss 
Will not sell again, until much higher 
Trapped bulls 
exit with loss 
Will not buy again, 
until much lower

@Albrooksall

At Bottom: Strong Bulls Buy Back Shorts, Then Get Long 
At bottom, strong bulls will buy back shorts, and reverse to long 
They will be buying from bears, 
who expect follow-through and MM down 
Strong enough buying to reverse to bull 
Then buy all of the way up 
BrooksTradingCourse.com

@Albrooksall

exit with loss, then not sell again until much higher 
Bulls want as oversold and climactic as possible, 
to create as big a bear bar as possible, 
and then as big a reversal up as possible 
At bottom, bulls want 
to buy as low as possible

so that bears will have to buy high 
Makes bull reversal bigger, and stronger 
Higher probability of even higher prices '

Then sell all of the way down

@Albrooksall

Why Bother Finding S/R in Trend? Inertia and the 80°/o Rule 
Trend: 80% of time, strong bear trend will not stop at support, 
(and strong bull trend will not stop at resistance) 
Reversal attempts in strong bear trend will fail at resistance 
Become bear flags 
Sell reversal down from bear trend line, 
at top of bear channel (resistance) 
Sell strong BO below prior low (Sell The Close Bear Trend), 
betting support will fail to create DB

for taking partial or full profits

Why Bother Finding S/R in TR? Inertia and the 80°/o Rule 
TR: 80% of time, BO attempts will reverse at S/R 
Traders expect BOs will fail, 
so they BLSHS: Buy Low, Sell High and Scalp

@Albrooksall

Top of TR is resistance 
Bulls take profits, bears sell reversals down, especially 2nd signals 
Bottom of TR is support 
Bears take profits, bulls buy reversals up, especially 2nd signals

@Albrooksall

Market Looks for Best Price: Probes Too Far up and Down 
Market exists to facilitate trading 
Market tries to maximize trading (number of trades and trading volume) 
Constantly searches for price, where most trading will take place 
Has to probe up and down, to find that range 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 9 
@Albrooksall

S/R constantly pulls market towards them 
Market probes to see if more buyers or sellers there 
Will one side give up? 
Will market BO to new level or reverse? 
Bears continuing to turn market down at resistance

Slide 10 
@Albrooksall

Their selling out of longs, and 
selling by bears created BO

Slide 11 
@Albrooksall

Trend Discovers When Far Enough: By Going Too Far 
Market needs to find out what prices are too high, and too low 
If too high or low, then trading stops 
Market then probes other direction 
Not enough buyers at this price 
Has to go lower to find buyers 
Market discovered that no one willing to sell at this price 
Needs to go higher to find sellers

Every Type of Chart: Different Representation of Same Forces 
EURUSD 200 ticks 
1 
Traders don't all use 5 min charts 
This chart has 200 ticks per bar, and has clear patterns 
Many different choices for each type chart 
Time charts: 1, 3, 5, 15, 60 min 
Tick charts: 50, 100, 1000 ticks per bar 
Other charts: range, Renko, line, etc. 
Because Forex S/R are less precise, 
traders are less concerned about 
exact prices, and types of charts

The less obvious the line is, 
the less important, 
since fewer computers will use it 
Trend line is often far from perfect in Broad Channels 
Redraw it as needed, as the trend progresses 
Just look for a best fit 
Want a lot of touches, 
that captures the overall trend 
Higher probability of profit, 
if wait to sell BO, or close of 
strong, consecutive big bear bars

Reversals at Prior Highs and Lows: DTs & Bottoms 
Bears will sell reversal down from prior high 
Creates OT reversal 
0 
Bulls will buy reversal up from prior low 
Creates DB reversal

Slide 16 
@Albrooksall

Prior High or Low Often Tested after BO: BO PBs 
Bears will sell as PB reverses down, 
from BO point, creating a BO test 
Reversal after BO test is caused, 
in part, by scale in traders 
They scalp out with profit, or 
to avoid loss 
See Video on Scaling In 
Exit since possible 2nd Leg Down, 
after Wedge Top and Buy Climax, 
and now small Triangle 
Bulls will buy as PB reverses up, 
from BO point, creating a BO test

Sell at Resistance: Like Round Numbers in Bear Trend 
Emini 5 min 
Round numbers are S/R 
Most round numbers are option strike prices 
Emini is about 10x SPY, so Emini 2110 is close to SPY 211 
Every 10 points in Emini is round number S/R 
• 
Traders often put stops, and 
take profits at round numbers 
Institutions sell options, so want them to lose value 
They trade options, and underlying, to make options expire worthless 
This means at strike prices, so strike prices are magnets (S/R)

Daily Charts: Round Numbers (100 pips) 
EURGBP day 
Round numbers (100 or 1,000 pips) are also S/R 
0.88000 
Traders often put stops, and take profits at round numbers 
.. - .. 
Most round numbers are option strike prices, 
for currency futures options, and Forex options (not widely traded) 
Institutions sell options, so want them to lose value 
They trade options, and underlying, to make options expire worthless 
This means at strike prices, so strike prices are magnets {S/R)

@Albrooksall

' 
In strong bull trends, 
''Buy The Close Bulls'' buy strong bull closes, 
betting that they are a sign of higher prices, 
even if there is a PB first 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

' 
If a PB is disappointing, 
(if the Selling Pressure is too strong), 
bulls assume the trend is converting into a TR 
They add to their position as the bull resumes 
Scale into their long position 
Now TR, so Buy Low! 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 3 
@Albrooksall

Disappointed Bulls: Now Trade Like TR 
' 
Bulls believe the bull trend is over, 
and now in a TR 
Try to exit entire position at the first entry price 
TR, so Sell High, and Scalp! 
They will make a profit on the 2nd entry, 
and get out breakeven on the 1st buy 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 4 
@Albrooksall

Bulls Sell at Highest Close: So It Is Now Resistance 
' 
Bulls have limit orders to sell at highest close 
trt 
Whenever bulls sell, creates resistance 
Bears will short 
Bulls place a limit order, to sell both at the original entry 
This means that bulls are selling the next rally 
Their limit orders to sell at the close of the 1st entry bar, 
creates resistance at that level 
Often leads to a leg down 
Highest close of a bull trend, then becomes resistance 
Often leads to a leg down in the new TR 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 5 
@Albrooksall

In strong bear trends, 
''Sell The Close Bears'' sell strong bear closes 
Bet that they are a sign of lower prices, 
even if there is a PB first 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 6 
@Albrooksall

They add to their position as the bear resumes 
They scale into their short position 
TR, so Sell High! 
Here, maybe on strong bull close, 
or 1 - 2x size of scalp 
If a PB is disappointing, 
{if the Buying Pressure is too strong), 
bears assume the trend is converting into a TR

Slide 7 
@Albrooksall

Disappointed Bears: Now Trade Like TR 
Bears now believe the bear trend is over, 
and the market is now in a TR 
Try to exit entire position at the first entry price 
TR, so Buy Low, and Scalp to cover! 
They will try to make a profit on the 2nd entry, 
and get out breakeven on the first 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 8 
@Albrooksall

Bear Trend: Bears Scale In (Add to Position) 
Some bears will scale in again for a 3rd entry 
So many bears wanted to cover (buy), 
that the dip could not reach the lowest close 
Eager bulls and bears bought, so 2nd Leg Up likely 
Bears took profits 
They will make a profit on the 2nd entry, 
and get out breakeven on the 1st sell 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 9 
@Albrooksall

Bears Buy at Lowest Close: So It Is Now Support 
Bears have limit orders to buy at lowest close 
They place a limit order to buy back 
entire position at the original entry 
This means that bears are buying the next selloff 
Their limit orders to buy at the close of the 1st sell entry bar, 
creates support at that level 
The lowest close of a bear trend, then becomes support 
Often leads to a leg up in the new TR 
Whenever bears want to buy, 
creates support 
Bulls also buy

it is resistance

Breakeven on 1st entry 
Profit on 2nd buy

Minor Reversal: Often Called ''Noise," but Noise Cannot Exist 
Minor reversal is often called ''Noise'' 
Noise simply means something 
so small that you will ignore it 
Implies the move is random 
Bad term since nothing is random 
Somethings are too small for you to trade 
But, everything happens because of 
logical trades from computers 
Noise cannot exist

and start of bull trend

Head and Shoulders Top (HST): Always Is a LH MTR 
Left 
shoulder 
HH MTR 
HST is just a type of HH MTR 
where there is a left shoulder 
The HH MTR is followed by a LH MTR 
LH MTR 
Bull trend needs Higher Highs 
If turns down from Lower High, 
bull trend might have ended 
Bulls failed to make new high, so weak bulls

where there is a left shoulder

Bears failed to make new low, so weak bears

shoulder

■ What is a Final Flag?

Most Reversal Patterns: lnvolv,e Trading Ranges 
• Major Trend Reversals 
• Final Flags and other failed BOs 
• Wedges

Final Flag (FF): Look for the Last Flag in a Trend 
Trends have many flags and breakouts 
After trend reverses, can look back and 
find Final Flag in trend 
Example: Final Bull Flag 
in bull trend before market 
reverses into bear trend 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com

@Albrooksall

Small Trends: Often End with Final Flags 
Any trend, even small leg in Trading Range, 
can end with Final Flag reversal 
BO of Final Flag often does 
not reach new extreme 
Bear breakout below Final Bear Flag, 
but breakout held above bear low 
Big Final Flag 
Bull breakout below Final Bull Flag, 
but breakout held below bull high

Wedge: Can Be Flag or Major Trend Reversal 
A bull channel is almost always a bear flag on a higher time frame 
At a minimum, a bull channel evolves into a TR 75% of time, 
and is therefore a bull leg in what will become a TR 
Wedge Bear Flag 
is a Pullback in bear trend 
A bear flag is a small TR or bull trend 
A BO below a Wedge Bear Flag 
is a reversal of that small bull trend 
Wedge Top 
is a Reversal of bull trend

Wedge Reversals: Why Do They Work? 
1 
Goal of market is to find fair price 
Constantly probes up and down until clearly too far, 
then reverses and probes opposite direction 
If 3 pushes down and legs get smaller, 
and PB overlaps prior leg, 
market sees more Buying Pressure 
Less interest in lower prices

3

Wedge Reversals: Why Do They Work? 
1 
Once bulls and bears agree market fell too far, 
two legs up Ii ke ly 
Many trapped bulls and bears after first reversal up 
Bears decide too low to sell 
Now will only sell higher 
Bulls believe market fell too low, and they buy

If Bulls Too Eager to Buy Wedge: Get Undershoot 
If bulls and bears confident 
that new low will create Wedge Bottom, 
many bulls will buy early, just in case get HL instead of new L 
Might be so many buyers that 3rd push down 
might not fall below bottom of 2nd push down (undershoot) 
Bears have same doubt and buy back shorts above prior low

Failed Double Top or Bottom: 3 Pushes So Type of Wedge 
Breakout above Double Top that fails 
and reverses down has 3 pushes up 
Computers trade it like a type of Wedge 
Bears sell the Double Top as it turns down and exit above 
Bulls buy the breakout above, and bears buy back shorts 
3 
If market turns down a third time, usually gets swing down

Wedge: Need Strong Legs and Good Context 
Three pushes are not enough 
They have to be strong compared to 
the other bars and legs 
Context has to make sense for reversal 
Otherwise, sideways is more likely than reversal 
If pattern does not look right, computers ignore it 
Traders will call it something else 
When reversals are around same price, 
pattern is Triple Top or Triple Bottom 
Tight Trading Range 
Legs 1 and 3 are too weak 
Computers will ignore 
No strong BO up or down

Strong bears always forming Wedge Bottoms 
Legs don't overlap (Gaps) 
so bear trend too strong to buy 
No strong bull trend bars (Buying Pressure) 
Lines not convergent 
The stronger the bear trend, 
the stronger the buy signal bar has to be 
No strong buy signal bars here 
Tight Bear Channel is bear BO 
on higher time frame 
Only sell and do not look to buy 
Reversals are simply profit taking 
Not attempt at bull trend

Bad Wedges: Present in Most Strong Trends 
Strong bull trend always forms Wedge Tops 
Create by minor profit taking by bulls and 
from weak bears hoping for PB that is big enough for scalp 
80% of reversal attempts fail and become bull flags, 
and bear scalpers keep losing 
Tight Bull Channel is bull BO 
on higher time frame 
Only buy and do not look to sell

Channel is tight, not broad 
No big consecutive bull trend bars so 
not enough Buying Pressure for reversal 
Convergent lines, but Gap below 1st leg 
Wedge Bottom usually needs

Gap means strong bears

so bulls are too weak to create reversal up

Reversal Patterns: Involve Trading Ranges 
■ Major Trend Reversals 
■ Final Flags and other failed BOs 
■ Wedges 
■ Double Tops and Bottoms 
• Triangles 
■ Head and shoulders patterns 
■ Rounding bottoms and tops 
■ Climaxes

Double Top (DT) or Double Bottom (DB): Flag or Reversal 
OT and DB are two of most common patterns 
Many on every chart 
Often 2 - 3 ways to draw the lines 
Most are too small to trade profitably 
Many are good swing and scalp setups 
Can be PB in trend 
or reversal at end of trend 
+ 
because the computers are! 
Computers simply looking for 
test of prior high or low 
Does not matter if not perfect

DT: Why Does It Often Lead to Reversal Down? 
Some bull bought the high, expecting higher prices, 
or at least a minor reversal and TR, not a bear trend 
Bull sells out at original entry price to avoid loss 
l~' 
(scale in bulls make profit) 
I 
~•,o,. 
,t~ t 
Disappointed by big selloff 
Now thinks TR 
Some buy more above bull bar 
Look to get out around 1st buy 
+ 
Bulls selling along with bears 
Creates reversal and DT 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 9 
@Albrooksall

DB: Why Does It Often Lead to Reversal Up? 
Some bear sold the low, 
expecting lower prices, 
Disappointed by big rally 
Now thinks TR 
Some sell more below bar 
Look to get out around 1st sell 
or at least a minor reversal and TR, 
not a bull trend 
+ Bears buying along with bulls 
Creates reversal 
and DB (here, HL DB) 
original entry price to avoid loss 
Instead, bought 2nd entry buy 
since now major reversal, 
even though at loss

Tests of Support: Often Have Many DBs 
Markets always looking for confirmation 
If turned up at a price level, 
market wants to confirm it is strong support 
Sells off back to that level to see if turns up again 
DB 
DB 
DB 
Sometimes tests same area many times 
and continues to find support 
Bulls buying each selloff, confident of bottom 
Bears taking profits because 
think bear breakout unlikely 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

DT Reversal: 2nd High Is Test of 1st High 
If turned down at a price level, 
market wants to confirm 
it is strong resistance 
OT 
(Also ET 
so HH MTR) 
Q6t't 
Q++D■!rr 
O 
Markets always looking for confirmation 
Confirmation increases probability 
If rallies back to that level, 
see if turns down again 
Bears look to sell reversal down 
Bulls hope for bull BO with 
follow-through to buy 
Too much selling on reversal down 
Bulls gave up 
High probability results in trend of at least TBTL (Ten Bars, Two legs)

Triangle: A Type of Tight Trading Range (TTR) 
Triangle is a Tight Trading Range (TTR) 
where there are at least 3 legs in at least one direction 
As soon as 3 pushes up or down, it is a Triangle 
If not clear, just call it a TTR 
If clear, can call it a Triangle

Expanding Triangle (ET): All Big Ones Are MTRs 
Expanding Triangle (ET) is a Triangle that gets wider 
Some call it a Megaphone 
4 
2 
1 
As soon as 5 reversals, 
it is a Triangle 
If so, 2nd or 4th leg 
3 
always breaks trend line and tests MA 
5 
ET Top often follows ET Bottom 
If not in Tight Trading Range (TTR), and 
legs 4 or 5 have at least 5 big bars 
or 10 small bars, 
ET is also MTR 
~ 
~, 
Buy reversal up 
ET Bottom so LL MTR

Computers: Ignore Bad Looking Patterns 
Closer pattern is to ideal, 
more likely it will work 
~□~~ 
1 
3 
Bad shape (bad symmetry, bar proportions) 
Only 2 bars in Leg 2, then 50 bars in Leg 4 
More of a TTR than an ET 
Less it resembles ideal, 
less likely it will influence what follows 
Both choices for Leg 5 more likely bear flags in bear trend 
5 
Computers ignore bad patterns 
Computers will see it as something else, not as ET 
Bad looking patterns are low probability 
and as bad as having no pattern at all

Every HST is a TR

~ 
Some traders sell the BO below the neck line, 
but lose most of time 
Most Trading Range breakouts fail and reverse up, like here 
If reversal down from right shoulder is a strong bear BO {not here), 
selling the BO below the neck line has a better chance of being profitable 
Usually better to sell reversal down from right shoulder (LH MTR), 
so selling near top of range (BLSHS)

Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

Markets Resist Change: Often Need 2nd or 3rd Attempt 
3 ways to draw neck line 
Usually all about the same level 
Horizontal line from bottom of selloff from head 
Horizontal line from bottom of selloff from left shoulder 
Trend line connecting those lows 
Rarely, lines far apart 
Look at highest one first 
If market falls below, then look to see 
if reverses up from next neck line

@Albrooksall

Markets Resist Change: Often Need 2nd or 3rd Attempt 
Markets have inertia and resist change 
When market tries to reverse, 1st attempt often fails 
Often needs several more attempts before successful reversal

Slide 
■ What is a climax? 
3 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 1 
@Albrooksall

Most Reversal Patterns: lnvolv!e Trading Ranges 
■ Major Trend Reversals 
■ Final Flags and other failed BOs 
■ Wedges 
■ Double Tops and Bottoms 
■ Triangles 
■ Head and Shoulders patterns 
■ Rounded Tops and Bottoms 
■ Climaxes 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

Every trend bar is also a BO and a climax 
The term climax is a BO that ends a leg or trend 
Leads to TR or opposite trend 
It often is an acceleration of a move up or down 
Traders do not refer to a BO as a climax 
until the following bars show 
l that the trend has halted 
~~1.JJ'o1-
1W 
50% chance of trend resumption 
50% chance of trend reversal 
Most climaxes lead to 3 - 10 bar TR 
Then, the trend can reverse or resume 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 3 
@Albrooksall

Climax: Acceleration of a Move up or Down 
BO usually fails and is therefore at least a small climax 
Most are only a single bar 
Most lead to minor reversal 
(either a PB in a trend or a leg in a TR) 
Successful BO also usually only leads to 
minor leg in trend or TR 
Buy Climax 
Bulls becoming aggressive 
Bears giving up 
Can be brief or last for many bars 
Sell Climax 
Bears becoming aggressive 
Bulls giving up 
Can be brief or last for many bars 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 4 
@Albrooksall

Every Trend Bar or Series of T'rend Bars: Climax 
Successful BO sometimes leads to swing 
Becomes a Measuring Gap and start of new trend 
BO usually fails and is climactic end of leg or trend 
Failed BO Exhaustion Gap and climax 
Gap between 
BO point and PB 
Gap between 
Close of bear bar and 
low of BP point (and 
low of prior bar) 
BO usually fails and becomes an Exhaustion Gap 
Usually reverses and tests at least to top of Sell Climax 
Failed BO usually becomes PB (here, bear flag) 
Some lead to opposite swings or trends 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 5 
@Albrooksall

Unusually strong surge late in trend 
Then, abrupt, unexpected (low probability) 
big reversal 
Climax and Climactic Reversal are 
two terms for same thing 
Fast reversal, no PB 
No chance to exit without big loss 
V Bottom 
V Top or 
Inverted V Top 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 6 
@Albrooksall

Climax is a trend bar or series of bars and a BO 
Extreme acceleration in move up or down 
Like all breakouts, 
can succeed and become trend 
Here, Buy Climax, but more rally 
o•.¢•• 
T'+++.+ot-+++-t-Ti 
96ot 
+tD 
Or, the BO can fail 
Here, Buy Climax, but reversal down 
Climactic Reversal 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 7 
@Albrooksall

Any unsustainable move, 
usually late in trend (after 20 or more bars) 
If trend continues, call it BO 
If trend pauses or reverses, 
call it climax or failed BO 
All markets constantly try to break out 
Every trend bar and tail is a BO and climax 
Most are brief and fail 
Every series of trend bars is a climax 
(but breakouts can go very far before ending) 
Climax usually evident on smaller time frame

Bull trend exhaustion 
Bull trend runs out of buyers at current price

usually leads to exhaustion 
No more buyers 
Buy vacuum is a Buy Climax 
that is testing resistance, usually at top of TR 
Single Buy Climax 
is usually test of resistance

and high of prior bar or last higher high 
Minimum goal of Climactic Reversal 
is temporary end of trend

Slide 4 
@Albrooksall

Buy Climax: No One Left to Buy at High Price 
After big, surprise BO (low probability event), bulls take profits 
Want to avoid risk of Climactic Reversal 
See big bar as gift 
Opportunity to take profits at a surprisingly high price 
There is no one left to buy at this high price 
Weak bears finally bought back shorts 
Strong bears will never buy back shorts 
Weak bulls not strong enough to continue rally 
Strong bulls only want to buy lower 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide S 
@Albrooksall

Buy Climax: Bulls Wait 10 or More Bars Before Buying Again 
Once bulls take profits, they do not look to buy again just 
1- 5 bars later and only a little lower 
If that was their intention, they would not have exited 
They took profits because of possible exhaustion and 
deep pullback or reversal 
Before buying again, they wait 10 or more bars and 
two or more legs to make sure bears not strong 
enough to reverse 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 6 
@Albrooksall

Bear breakout has a gap between close of 
bear bar and low of prior bar or last Lower Low 
Bears take profits because of risk 
of Climactic Reversal 
Bulls buy, hoping for reversal up

BrooksPriceAction.com

Climax Reversals: Usually Do Not Reverse 
Most Climactic Reversals do not reverse the trend 
Instead, usually just end the current move 
When Climactic Reversals are forming, 
they look strong and scary 
Look like they could quickly start an opposite strong trend 
In hindsight, usually just sharp leg in a TR 
Climactic Reversal up, 
then TR 
Climactic Reversal down, 
then TR

usually find climax 
on smaller time frame

Consecutive Sell Climax: Often Caused by Option Firms 
Some institutions specialize in selling options 
~•r 
' ~. 
t t.6 
+o+~Y 
If a stock falls at a steady rate, like 0.2% per hour, 
the delta of the Puts increases faster 
than 0.2% per hour, and it accelerates 
As a result, the institutions that sell Puts 
have losses that are accelerating 
This is called increasing gamma of their Puts, 
which means the delta is increasing parabolically 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 2 
@Albrooksall

Consecutive Sell Climax: Often Caused by Option Firms 
They have to hedge their losses 
Easiest way is to sell stocks and futures 
With their losses accelerating, they 
accelerate their selling of stocks and futures, 
adding to the Seti Climax 
As much as 50% of the Sell Climax can be caused 
by these institutions hedging 
O, ,.,T~0 l.T 
their short Puts 
Tu 
6t9T+•9 
'• + 09
9 
________,Q 9 
This is confirmed if reversal up or 
no follow-through selling tomorrow 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 3 
@Albrooksall

Consecutive Sell Climax: Often Caused by Option Firms 
Sell Climaxes slightly different from Buy Climaxes 
Most firms prefer to buy value 
Fewer are willing to buy strong rallies 
Buy Climaxes tend to be less impressive 
than Sell Climaxes 
Most firms that buy Calls are bulls 
They are buying Calls instead of stock 
to make a bet with smaller risk 
Firms that sell options do not have the same 
rate of gamma increase during Buy Climaxes 
as during Sell Climaxes 
Their pressure to hedge by 
buying stocks or futures is less 
Less urgent buying by both types of firms 
mean Buy Climaxes are not as extreme 
as Sell Climaxes 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com

@Albrooksall

Consecutive Sell Climax: PB Has About Half as Many Bars

it is still a 20 bar bear channel on a higher time frame 
TBTL correction is on the highest time frame 
that has the pattern 
In general, the minimum goal is about 
half as many bars as in the trend 
When bear channel lasts 30 - SO bars, 
bull reversal usually lasts at least 20 bars 
before bears will consider selling again 
Bulls will decide that price has become 
too high to buy

Traders need an edge, which is 
mathematical advantage 
Probably will make profit over time 
Zero sum game 
Stop 
Trying to take money from very smart people 
All trading is subjective and hard, 
but challenge is part of fun! 
Some look for low risk trades, 
but have low probability of profit 
Others wait for more certainty, but pay for it 
with more risk (stop for bears now far) and 
less remaining profit 
6

@Albrooksall

Trading Range so traders buying low, selling high, scalping 
HFT firms use algorithms for only 
days to weeks before replacing them 
Holy grail (perfect strategy) cannot last 
Smart people would soon discover it 
No one left to take other side 
Stops working 
Strong bear BO 
Can no longer buy low 
Bears should swing, not scalp 
_,.,. ...

{Spike and Channel 
Bear Trend) 
Tight Channel so only 
look to sell, not buy

@Albrooksall

Trading Range, but setups never clear so not high probability 
Perfect trade 
High probability 
Big reward 
Small risk 
Cannot exist because an institution has to take other side 
There is always something wrong with every trade 
No firm would take a trade where there is 
low probability of making only 
small reward while exposed to big risk 
Strong bear BO 
High probability short, 
but stop is far so risk is big 
Bear channel 
Probability still good, 
but less than in BO 
t 
Stop usually further than in TR 
so risk is not small

Slide 4 
@Albrooksall

Market in Cycle: Need to Keep Changing Strategy 
Once everyone agrees in TR, soon get BO 
TR strategy no longer works 
Market is in cycle 
Traders cannot expect any strategy to last long 
Once too many computers begin to do same thing, 
market advances to next stage in cycle 
Breakouts do not last long 
t 
Channel will soon evolve into TR 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 5 
@Albrooksall

When Almost Perfect: Not Enough Institutions on Other Side 
Early in bear BO, stop is not far and 
probability of lower prices is high 
Profit taking comes soon 
BO quickly replaced by channel 
Because institutions see this as a near perfect short, 
they all sell quickly 
Creates big BO, increasing risk, and 
reducing remaining reward and probability 
Sometimes becomes climactic failed bear BO that reverses up 
Entering in strong BO when 
context is good is 
close to perfection, 
but very brief opportunity 
t 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 6 
@Albrooksall

When setup approaches perfection, 
quickly traded by many institutions 
Strong BO is big bar because all institutions see it 
as nearly perfect opportunity 
When setup is close to perfect, get huge trend bar 
Greatly increases risk 
Reduces remaining reward and probability 
Can be climactic end of move 
t

BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 7 
@Albrooksall

Perfect Trades: Don't Need Perfection to Make Money 
No one will give you money 
Your edge is always small 
Goal is to be consistently good 
Don't have to be perfect 
Structure trades that make sense 
Use appropriate stops and exits 
Manage trades correctly 
t 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com 
Slide 8 
@Albrooksall

Skilled Traders: Base Every Trade on Math 
Traders always ask themselves, 
''Will I probably make money on this trade?'' 
Everyone, at least subconsciously, will only take a trade if 
chance of success times the reward is 
significantly greater than 
chance of failure times the risk 
t 
BrooksTradingCourse.com 
BrooksPriceAction.com

@Albrooksall

Si Ide 
2

Slide 10

Basis for All Trades: Trader's Equation 
Only take a trade if 
(Probability of win) x (Reward) > (Probability of loss) x (Risk) 
This is the Trader's Equation 
If positive, then makes mathematical sense to take trade 
Can lose 60% of time and still make money 
t

BrooksPriceAction.com

■ 3 variables

Slide 1
